subject: Chemistry
title: Jenner's Cowpox Experiment
description: A quick win: understand Jenner's Cowpox Experiment.

hook.text: In 1796, Edward Jenner injected a boy with cowpox pus, then exposed him to smallpox. The boy survived—and vaccines were born.

buildup.text: Milkmaids who caught cowpox never got smallpox. Jenner hypothesized that cowpox gave immunity to its deadly cousin.

discovery.text: Cowpox virus is similar enough to smallpox that the immune system's response to one protects against both.

twist.text: Jenner tested on an 8-year-old boy, James Phipps. By modern ethics, this experiment would be completely illegal.

climax.text: Smallpox killed 300 million people in the 1900s alone. Vaccination eradicated it by 1980—the only human disease ever eliminated.

punchline.text: Cowpox pus, one boy, one bold guess. The first vaccine beat humanity's worst killer.

quiz.question: Why did cowpox infection protect against smallpox?
options: The cowpox virus is similar enough that immunity to one protects against both
Cowpox killed smallpox viruses on contact inside the body
Cowpox permanently changed the DNA of immune cells
